Control Arm - N.evidence: The total number of participants included in the control arm (standard non-steroidal antiandrogen group) is reported multiple times in the document, including in the 'Findings' section ('n=562; control group'), the trial profile diagram ('562 assigned non-steroidal antiandrogen'), and Table 1 ('Control group (n=562)'). This number refers to the intention-to-treat population, which is the standard for reporting the size of the control arm in clinical trials.

Total Participants - N.evidence: The total number of participants included in the clinical trial is reported multiple times, including in the 'Findings' section ('1125 participants were randomly assigned to receive non-steroidal antiandrogen (n=562; control group) or enzalutamide (n=563)'), in the 'Methods' section ('1125 enrolled and randomly assigned'), and in the 'Results' section ('1125 participants were randomly assigned to the control group (n=562) or the enzalutamide group (n=563; intention-to-treat population)'). This number includes all participants in either the treatment or control arm.

Number of Arms Included.evidence: The document describes a randomized, phase 3 trial in which participants were randomly assigned (1:1) to receive either testosterone suppression plus enzalutamide (enzalutamide group) or testosterone suppression plus a weaker non-steroidal antiandrogen (control group). This is confirmed in the methods section and in the trial profile (Figure 1), which shows 562 assigned to the control group and 563 to the enzalutamide group. Thus, there are two arms: one experimental (enzalutamide) and one control (non-steroidal antiandrogen).

Treatment Arm 1 Regimen.value: Testosterone suppression plus oral enzalutamide (160 mg once per day), with or without concurrent docetaxel (75 mg/m² intravenously for up to six cycles every 3 weeks, at the discretion of participants and physicians).
Treatment Arm 1 Regimen.evidence: The Methods section states that participants were randomly assigned to receive testosterone suppression plus oral enzalutamide (160 mg once per day) or a weaker standard oral non-steroidal antiandrogen (control group). Concurrent docetaxel (75 mg/m² intravenously for up to six cycles every 3 weeks) was allowed at the discretion of participants and physicians. This regimen is confirmed in the Procedures section and throughout the Results and Discussion, which describe the enzalutamide group as receiving testosterone suppression plus enzalutamide, with or without docetaxel.

Treatment Arm - N.evidence: The number of participants included in the experimental arm (enzalutamide group) is reported multiple times in the document, including in the trial profile (Figure 1) and Table 1: '563 assigned enzalutamide', '563 included in ITT analysis', and '563 received at least one dose of treatment'. The intention-to-treat (ITT) population, which is standard for reporting clinical trial results, is 563 for the enzalutamide (experimental) arm.

Class of Agent in Treatment Arm 1.value: enzalutamide (a potent androgen receptor inhibitor) plus testosterone suppression
Class of Agent in Treatment Arm 1.evidence: The Methods section states that participants in the first experimental arm received 'testosterone suppression plus oral enzalutamide (160 mg once per day)'. The Introduction and Discussion clarify that enzalutamide is a potent androgen receptor inhibitor, and testosterone suppression is standard androgen deprivation therapy. Thus, the class of agent is 'enzalutamide (a potent androgen receptor inhibitor) plus testosterone suppression'.
Class of Agent in Treatment Arm 1.group_name: Class of Agent in Treatment Arm 1

Treatment Arm(s).value: Testosterone suppression plus enzalutamide; Testosterone suppression plus standard non-steroidal antiandrogen therapy (bicalutamide, nilutamide, or flutamide)
Treatment Arm(s).evidence: The methods section states participants were randomly assigned to receive testosterone suppression plus oral enzalutamide (160 mg once per day) or a weaker standard oral non-steroidal antiandrogen (bicalutamide, nilutamide, or flutamide; control group). Table 1 and Figure 1 also refer to these as the 'Enzalutamide group' and 'Control group (non-steroidal antiandrogen)'.

Control Arm.value: testosterone suppression plus standard non-steroidal antiandrogen therapy (bicalutamide, nilutamide, or flutamide)
Control Arm.evidence: The control arm is described multiple times, including in the Methods section: 'Participants were randomly assigned (1:1)... to receive testosterone suppression plus oral enzalutamide (160 mg once per day) or a weaker standard oral non-steroidal antiandrogen (bicalutamide, nilutamide, or flutamide; control group) until clinical disease progression or prohibitive toxicity.' The control group is also referred to as the 'non-steroidal antiandrogen group' throughout the document, including in Table 1 and Figure 2.

Quality of Life reported.evidence: In the Outcomes section, it is stated that 'health-related quality of life (HRQOL)' was a secondary endpoint and 'The HRQOL results have been reported elsewhere¹⁸'. In the Discussion, it is further noted that 'The short-term adverse event profile associated with enzalutamide (with and without docetaxel use) and the impact on quality of life have been reported previously. 6.18 In these reports, a modest decrease in quality of life with early enzalutamide compounded with the addition of docetaxel was reported. However, after 6 months there was no further incremental decline in quality of life.' This confirms that quality of life results were collected and reported, though in a separate publication.

Median Follow-Up Duration (mo).evidence: The median follow-up duration is explicitly reported in the Findings section: 'After a median follow-up of 68 months (IQR 67–69)...' and reiterated in the Results section: 'At data cutoff (Jan 19, 2022), 476 deaths had occurred after a median follow-up of 68 months (IQR 67–69).' This value refers to the whole study population.

Volume of disease - N (%) | High | Treat.evidence: Table 1 (Baseline characteristics) reports under 'Volume of disease' for the Enzalutamide group (treatment): High: 301 (53%).

Volume of disease - N (%) | High | Contr.evidence: Table 1 (Baseline characteristics) reports under 'Volume of disease' for the Control group: High: 301 (54%).

Volume of disease - N (%) | Low | Contro.evidence: Table 1 (Baseline characteristics) reports under 'Volume of disease' for the Control group: Low: 261 (46%).

Median On-Treatment Duration (mo) | Trea.evidence: The Results section states: 'Median time on protocol therapy was 58 months (IQR 49–67) for the enzalutamide group and 23 months (21–25) for the control group.' This refers to the median duration participants received on-treatment therapy in the enzalutamide (treatment) arm.

Median On-Treatment Duration (mo) | Cont.evidence: The Results section states: 'Median time on protocol therapy was 58 months (IQR 49–67) for the enzalutamide group and 23 months (21–25) for the control group.' This refers to the median duration participants received on-treatment therapy in the control arm.

Trial.evidence: The name of the clinical study is stated multiple times in the document, including in the title ('Testosterone suppression plus enzalutamide versus testosterone suppression plus standard antiandrogen therapy for metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (ENZAMET): an international, open-label, randomised, phase 3 trial') and in the methods section ('The ENZAMET (Australian and New Zealand Urogenital and Prostate Cancer Trials Group [ANZUP] 1304) open-label, randomised, phase 3 trial').

Docetaxel administration - N (%) | Treat.evidence: Table 1 (Baseline characteristics) reports 'Actual use of early docetaxel*' as 243 (43%) in the Enzalutamide group (treatment arm). This represents the number and percentage of participants in the treatment arm who received docetaxel before or during the trial.

Docetaxel administration - N (%) | Contr.evidence: Table 1 (Baseline characteristics) reports 'Actual use of early docetaxel*' as 240 (43%) in the Control group. This represents the number and percentage of participants in the control arm who received docetaxel before or during the trial.

Mode of metastases - N (%) | Synchronous.evidence: Table 1 (Baseline characteristics) reports under 'Metastatic status at first diagnosis' for the Enzalutamide group (treatment): 'M1 (synchronous)‡' as 335 (60%).
Table 1 (Baseline characteristics) reports under 'Metastatic status at first diagnosis' for the Control group: 'M1 (synchronous)‡' as 348 (62%).

Mode of metastases - N (%) | Metachronou.evidence: Table 1 (Baseline characteristics) reports under 'Metastatic status at first diagnosis' for the Enzalutamide group (treatment): 'M0 (metachronous)§' as 157 (28%).
Table 1 (Baseline characteristics) reports under 'Metastatic status at first diagnosis' for the Control group: 'M0 (metachronous)§' as 158 (28%).

Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | S.evidence: The document repeatedly states that results were reported for synchronous metastatic disease as a prognostic subgroup. For example, the Summary and Methods sections specify analyses by 'synchronous and metachronous high-volume or low-volume disease.' Figure 4A and 4C, as well as multiple text references, present overall survival and other outcomes specifically for synchronous disease.

Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | M.evidence: The document explicitly reports results for metachronous metastatic disease as a prognostic subgroup. The Summary, Methods, and Results sections, as well as Figure 4B and multiple text references, present outcomes for metachronous disease, including overall survival and subgroup analyses.

Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | H.evidence: High-volume disease is a prespecified and reported subgroup throughout the document. Table 1, Results, and Discussion sections, as well as Figure 4C, provide data and analyses for high-volume disease, both in synchronous and metachronous settings.

Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | L.evidence: Low-volume disease is also a prespecified and reported subgroup. Table 1, Results, and Discussion sections, as well as Figure 4C, provide data and analyses for low-volume disease, both in synchronous and metachronous settings.

NCT.evidence: The Methods section states: 'This study is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT02446405, ANZCTR, ACTRN12614000110684, and EudraCT, 2014-003190-42.'

PubMed ID.evidence: PubMed ID is not reported anywhere in the provided document.

Trial Name.evidence: The title and multiple sections refer to the trial as 'ENZAMET'.

Author.evidence: The author list at the beginning of the article starts with 'Christopher J Sweeney, Andrew J Martin, Martin R Stockler, ... for the ENZAMET trial investigators* and Australian and New Zealand Urogenital and Prostate Cancer Trials Group'. The 'et al.' format is appropriate for this long list.

Year.evidence: The citation at the top and bottom of the article states 'Lancet Oncol 2023; 24: 323-34' and 'Copyright © 2023 Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.'

Full Pub or Abstract.evidence: The document is a full publication from The Lancet Oncology, not just an abstract.

Phase.evidence: The title and multiple sections (e.g., 'ENZAMET: an international, open-label, randomised, phase 3 trial') specify this is a phase 3 trial.

Original/Follow Up.evidence: This is the planned primary overall survival analysis of the ENZAMET trial, not a follow-up or secondary analysis.

Quality of Life Scale.evidence: The document states that health-related quality of life (HRQOL) was a secondary endpoint and that the HRQOL results have been reported elsewhere (see reference 18: Stockler MR, Martin AJ, Davis ID, et al. J Clin Oncol 2022; 40: 837–46). The current document does not provide any specific quality of life scale values, scores, or detailed results. It only mentions that a modest decrease in quality of life with early enzalutamide compounded with docetaxel was reported previously, and that after 6 months there was no further incremental decline in quality of life, but no quantitative data or scale is provided.

This is also supported by the 'Methods' and 'Summary' sections, which repeatedly refer to overall survival as the main outcome measured in the trial.

Secondary Endpoint(s).value: PSA progression-free survival (defined as time from randomisation to the earliest PSA progression according to the Prostate Cancer Working Group 2 criteria [PCWG2], clinical progression, or death from any cause, whichever occurred first, or the last known date of follow-up without PSA progression), clinical progression-free survival (defined as the earliest sign of radiographic progression using PCWG2 for bone lesions and RECIST [version 1.1] for soft tissue lesions, symptoms attributable to cancer progression, or initiation of another anti-cancer treatment for prostate cancer), health-related quality of life (HRQOL), health outcomes relative to costs, and safety.
Secondary Endpoint(s).evidence: Explicitly listed in the Outcomes section: 'The primary endpoint was overall survival... The secondary endpoints were PSA progression-free survival..., clinical progression-free survival..., health-related quality of life (HRQOL), health outcomes relative to costs, and safety.'

TTPSA (mo) | Treatment.evidence: Figure 2B (Kaplan-Meier curves) reports 'PSA progression-free survival' for the enzalutamide group (treatment arm) as median 68.0 months (95% CI 59.0-81.0). This matches the definition of TTPSA (time to PSA progression) for the treatment arm.

TTPSA (mo) | Control.evidence: Figure 2B (Kaplan-Meier curves) reports 'PSA progression-free survival' for the control group as median 22.0 months (95% CI 20.0-25.0). This matches the definition of TTPSA (time to PSA progression) for the control arm.

No. of Deaths - N | Treatment.evidence: The number of deaths in the enzalutamide (treatment) group is reported in multiple locations, including the Results section: 'As of data cutoff, 208 (37%) of 563 participants in the enzalutamide group ... had died.' This is also confirmed in the subgroup analysis chart: 'Overall 268/562 208/563', where 208 deaths occurred in the enzalutamide group.

No. of Deaths - N | Control.evidence: The number of deaths in the control group is reported in multiple locations, including the Results section: 'As of data cutoff, ... 268 (48%) of 562 in the control group had died.' This is also confirmed in the subgroup analysis chart: 'Overall 268/562 208/563', where 268 deaths occurred in the control group.

PFS Rate (%) | Overall | Treatment.value: 5-year PSA progression-free survival: 54% (95% CI 49–58); Hazard ratio for PSA progression-free survival: 0.44 (95% CI 0.38–0.52)
PFS Rate (%) | Overall | Treatment.evidence: Figure 2B and text in Results section report that the 5-year PSA progression-free survival in the enzalutamide group (treatment) was 54% (95% CI 49–58), with a hazard ratio for PSA progression-free survival of 0.44 (95% CI 0.38–0.52) compared to control. This is for the overall intention-to-treat population.

PFS Rate (%) | Overall | Control.evidence: Figure 2B and text in Results section report that the 5-year PSA progression-free survival in the control group was 25% (95% CI 22–29). This is for the overall intention-to-treat population.

Adverse Events - N (%) | All-Cause Grade.evidence: From the Results section: 'Ten (2%) of 558 participants died due to serious adverse events and 183 (33%) had serious grade 3 or 4 events in the control group whereas 13 (2%) of 563 participants died due to serious adverse events and 264 (47%) had serious grade 3 or 4 events in the enzalutamide group.' The enzalutamide group is the treatment arm. 264/563 = 47%.

From the Results section: 'Ten (2%) of 558 participants died due to serious adverse events and 183 (33%) had serious grade 3 or 4 events in the control group.' The control group had 183/558 = 33% with grade 3 or 4 events.

Adverse Events - N (%) | Treatment-relat.evidence: From the Results section: 'Discontinuation due to treatment-related adverse events occurred in 25 (5%) of 558 participants who received standard non-steroidal antiandrogen and 63 (11%) of 563 participants who received enzalutamide.' This is the only explicit reporting of treatment-related grade 3 or higher events (as discontinuations due to treatment-related AEs).

From the Results section: 'Discontinuation due to treatment-related adverse events occurred in 25 (5%) of 558 participants who received standard non-steroidal antiandrogen.' This is the only explicit reporting of treatment-related grade 3 or higher events (as discontinuations due to treatment-related AEs).

From the Summary: 'No deaths were attributed to study treatment.' Also, in the Results: 'No deaths were attributed to enzalutamide.' Thus, treatment-related grade 5 events (deaths) in the enzalutamide group are 0.
From the Summary: 'No deaths were attributed to study treatment.' Thus, treatment-related grade 5 events (deaths) in the control group are 0.

COE_RCT_IND_OVERALL_RJ.evidence: The document provides detailed results of the ENZAMET randomized controlled trial, including efficacy and safety outcomes, subgroup analyses, and adverse events. However, it does not report an explicit overall risk of bias judgment or individual criteria of evidence (COE) risk judgment for the RCT. There is no table, figure, or text section that summarizes the overall risk of bias or COE for the trial as a whole or for individual domains (e.g., randomization, blinding, outcome assessment).

Median PFS (mo) | Overall | Treatment.evidence: Figure 2B (Kaplan-Meier curves) reports 'PSA progression-free survival' for the enzalutamide group (treatment): median 68.0 months (95% CI 59.0-81.0). This is the overall ITT population.

Median PFS (mo) | Overall | Control.evidence: Figure 2B (Kaplan-Meier curves) reports 'PSA progression-free survival' for the control group: median 22.0 months (95% CI 20.0-25.0). This is the overall ITT population.

Add-on Treatment.value: enzalutamide, docetaxel, standard non-steroidal antiandrogen (bicalutamide, nilutamide, or flutamide), bone antiresorptive therapy
Add-on Treatment.evidence: The trial compared testosterone suppression plus enzalutamide versus testosterone suppression plus standard non-steroidal antiandrogen (bicalutamide, nilutamide, or flutamide). Concurrent docetaxel was allowed for up to six cycles at the discretion of participants and physicians, and planned use of bone antiresorptive therapy was a stratification factor. These therapies were given in combination with the primary treatment (testosterone suppression) as additional interventions, as described in the Methods and Table 1.

Treatment Class.value: Testosterone suppression plus enzalutamide or testosterone suppression plus standard non-steroidal antiandrogen therapy (bicalutamide, nilutamide, or flutamide), with or without concurrent docetaxel
Treatment Class.evidence: The Methods section and Summary state that participants were randomly assigned to receive testosterone suppression plus oral enzalutamide or a weaker standard oral non-steroidal antiandrogen (bicalutamide, nilutamide, or flutamide; control group) until clinical disease progression or prohibitive toxicity. Concurrent docetaxel was allowed for up to six cycles at the discretion of participants and physicians. This categorization is consistent throughout the document, including in the trial profile and baseline characteristics table.

ORR - N (%) | Treatment | Overall.evidence: No data on overall response rate (ORR: complete or partial response) for the enzalutamide (treatment) arm is reported in the main text, tables, or figures. The endpoints and results focus on overall survival, PSA progression-free survival, and clinical progression-free survival, but not radiographic response rates.

Type of Therapy.value: Testosterone suppression plus oral enzalutamide (160 mg once per day) or testosterone suppression plus a weaker standard oral non-steroidal antiandrogen (bicalutamide, nilutamide, or flutamide; control group), with or without concurrent docetaxel (75 mg/m² intravenously for up to six cycles every 3 weeks) at the discretion of participants and physicians.
Type of Therapy.evidence: The Methods section describes the interventions: participants were randomly assigned to receive testosterone suppression plus enzalutamide or testosterone suppression plus a weaker non-steroidal antiandrogen (bicalutamide, nilutamide, or flutamide). Concurrent docetaxel was allowed for up to six cycles at the discretion of participants and physicians. This is confirmed in the summary, methods, and procedures sections, and is consistent throughout the document for the whole study population.

OS Rate (%) | Overall | Treatment.evidence: In the Findings section and Figure 2A, it is stated: '5-year overall survival of 57% (0.53–0.61) in the control group and 67% (0.63–0.70) in the enzalutamide group.' The enzalutamide group is the experimental (treatment) arm.

OS Rate (%) | Overall | Control.evidence: In the Findings section and Figure 2A, it is stated: '5-year overall survival of 57% (0.53–0.61) in the control group and 67% (0.63–0.70) in the enzalutamide group.'

OS Rate (%) | Synchronous | Treatment.evidence: A specific 5-year OS rate for all synchronous patients in the treatment arm is not directly reported. Subgroup data for synchronous patients receiving docetaxel is available (60%), but not for all synchronous patients regardless of docetaxel.

OS Rate (%) | Synchronous | Control.evidence: A specific 5-year OS rate for all synchronous patients in the control arm is not directly reported. Subgroup data for synchronous patients receiving docetaxel is available (52%), but not for all synchronous patients regardless of docetaxel.

OS Rate (%) | Metachronous | Treatment.evidence: A specific 5-year OS rate for all metachronous patients in the treatment arm is not directly reported. Subgroup data for metachronous patients receiving docetaxel is not provided as a percentage.

OS Rate (%) | Metachronous | Control.evidence: A specific 5-year OS rate for all metachronous patients in the control arm is not directly reported. Subgroup data for metachronous patients receiving docetaxel is not provided as a percentage.

OS Rate (%) | High volume | Treatment.evidence: A specific 5-year OS rate for all high-volume patients in the treatment arm is not directly reported. The forest plot (subgroup analysis) provides hazard ratios but not OS rates as percentages.

OS Rate (%) | High volume | Control.evidence: A specific 5-year OS rate for all high-volume patients in the control arm is not directly reported. The forest plot (subgroup analysis) provides hazard ratios but not OS rates as percentages.

OS Rate (%) | Low volume | Treatment.evidence: A specific 5-year OS rate for all low-volume patients in the treatment arm is not directly reported. The forest plot (subgroup analysis) provides hazard ratios but not OS rates as percentages.

OS Rate (%) | Low volume | Control.evidence: A specific 5-year OS rate for all low-volume patients in the control arm is not directly reported. The forest plot (subgroup analysis) provides hazard ratios but not OS rates as percentages.
